name: Godot_Game_Creator
description: Full-stack game development skill enabling a Mind to design, build, test, and iterate on games using the Godot Engine. Manages the complete game development lifecycle from template selection through scene architecture, GDScript implementation, to automated playtesting with visual verification. Includes a template library for rapid prototyping — Minds match user intent to pre-built starter projects and customize from there, dramatically reducing time-to-playable.
progressive_unlock: Phase 1 (Immediate): Template browsing, project inspection, scene tree reading, script reading, debug output — allows Mind to discover templates and understand existing projects. Phase 2 (After successful inspection): Template cloning, scene creation, node manipulation, script creation/modification — enables building and editing from templates or scratch. Phase 3 (After successful build): Project execution, screenshot capture, input simulation, runtime scripting — enables testing and iteration. Full autonomous game creation loop unlocked after Mind demonstrates competence at each phase.

templateSystem.description: Pre-built Godot project templates that accelerate game creation. The Mind matches the user's natural language description to the closest template genre using keyword analysis, clones it as a starting point, then customizes scenes, scripts, and assets to match the user's specific vision.
matchingFlow: 1. Extract genre keywords from user's game description
2. Call Godot_ListTemplates to retrieve available templates
3. Score each template against extracted keywords using the keywords array in templateLibrary
4. If a template scores above 0.6 match confidence, use Godot_CloneTemplate to create a new project from it
5. If no template matches (score below 0.6), proceed with from-scratch creation at step 4 of the execution pipeline
6. After cloning, inspect the template project structure and identify customization points
7. Apply user-specific customizations (theme, mechanics, assets, difficulty) via existing edit tools

constraints: Always check for matching templates before building from scratch — template-first approach saves significant generation time
When using a template, always run Godot_GetSceneTree and Godot_ListProjectScripts on the cloned project to understand what's pre-built before making changes
Never modify template source files directly — always clone first via Godot_CloneTemplate to preserve the original template
Cache template match results in STREAM.entity to avoid redundant Godot_ListTemplates calls within the same session
Always save scenes after modifications using Godot_SaveScene before running
Never delete nodes without first reading the scene tree to understand dependencies
Validate GDScript syntax mentally before writing — check for proper indentation (tabs), signal connections, and type annotations
Always check debug output after running a project to catch errors early
Use screenshots after runtime changes to visually verify game state
Respect Godot naming conventions: PascalCase for nodes and classes, snake_case for variables and functions
Store project structure snapshots in STREAM.entity for quick reference across tool calls
Cache frequently-used scene paths and node paths in LTM with key: 'godot_project_{project_name}_paths'
When creating new scenes, always establish a clear node hierarchy before adding scripts
For 2D games, use Node2D-based root; for 3D games, use Node3D-based root; for UI, use Control-based root
Never execute Godot_RunScript with destructive operations (queue_free on root, file deletion) without Steward approval
Implement input simulation sequences incrementally — verify each step with screenshots before chaining complex sequences
When encountering script errors in debug output, read the affected script, fix the issue, save, and re-run before proceeding
Always create scripts in a dedicated 'res://scripts/' directory unless project conventions dictate otherwise
For scene organization, use 'res://scenes/' for game scenes, 'res://ui/' for interface scenes, 'res://levels/' for level data

steps.instruction: Validate Steward intent: determine if this is a new project, modification to existing project, or playtest request. Identify game type (2D, 3D, UI-heavy), genre, and scope. Extract genre keywords from the user's description.

Template matching: call Godot_ListTemplates to retrieve all available templates. Score each template against the user's genre keywords. If a template scores above 0.6 match confidence, proceed to step 2b. If no match, skip to step 3.

Template cloning: use Godot_CloneTemplate to create a new project from the matched template. This gives the Mind a fully functional starting project with pre-built scenes, scripts, mechanics, and placeholder assets.

Template inspection: use Godot_GetProjectInfo, Godot_GetSceneTree, and Godot_ListProjectScripts on the cloned project to understand what's pre-built. Identify which customization points from the template definition need to be addressed for the user's specific request. Store project map in STREAM.entity. Skip to step 5 (customization) — the template already has scenes, nodes, and base scripts.

For existing projects (no template): use Godot_GetProjectInfo to understand project structure, then Godot_ListProjectScenes and Godot_ListProjectScripts to map the codebase. Store project map in STREAM.entity.

For new projects from scratch (no template match): plan scene architecture. Create the main scene using Godot_CreateScene with appropriate root type. Design the node hierarchy on paper before building. Then use Godot_AddNode to construct the node hierarchy — structural nodes first (collision shapes, physics bodies), then visual nodes (sprites, meshes), then UI elements.

Customize and extend: whether from template or scratch, apply user-specific modifications. Swap placeholder assets with Godot_LoadSprite, modify node properties with Godot_EditNode (positions, colors, scales), add new nodes with Godot_AddNode, and adjust or create scripts. For template projects, focus on the customizationPoints listed in the template definition.

Write or modify game logic: use Godot_CreateScript for new behaviors or Godot_ModifyScript to update template scripts. Follow Godot patterns — extend the correct base class, implement _ready() for initialization, _process(delta) for per-frame logic, _physics_process(delta) for physics. Use proper signal connections for event handling.

Attach scripts to nodes: use Godot_AttachScript to bind new logic to scene elements. Verify the script's extends declaration matches the target node type. For template projects, scripts are already attached — only attach new scripts for added functionality.
Save all changes: call Godot_SaveScene for every modified scene before testing.
Run and test: use Godot_RunProject to execute the game. Immediately check Godot_GetDebugOutput for errors. If errors found, read affected scripts, fix issues, save, and re-run.

Visual verification: use Godot_TakeScreenshot to capture the game viewport. Analyze the screenshot to verify visual elements are positioned correctly, UI is rendering, and game state matches expectations.

Automated playtesting: use Godot_SimulateInput to execute gameplay sequences. Chain key presses, mouse clicks, and waits to simulate player behavior. Use Godot_GetUIElements to discover interactive elements for UI testing.
Live debugging: if runtime issues are found, use Godot_RunScript to execute diagnostic GDScript directly. Query node states, check variable values, and test fixes without restarting.

Iterate: based on visual feedback and debug output, modify scenes (Godot_EditNode, Godot_DeleteNode), update scripts (Godot_ModifyScript), and re-test. Continue the build-test-fix cycle until the game meets Steward specifications.

Finalize: stop the running project (Godot_StopProject), perform a final save of all scenes, and compile a summary of the game's architecture — scenes created, scripts written, key design decisions, template used (if any), and customizations applied. Push project summary to LTM for future reference.
